headlines.title: CITIZENS TO ACT AUG. 29 IN PETITION OF CONTEST
headlines.subtitle: Committee of Sixteen to Select Names to Go Upon Ticket.
headlines.fullText: CONTEST IN PROSPECT Rival Delegations Expected From Some Wards at Meeting Next Saturday. New sets of delegates from several wards are sure to appear in the convention for permanent organization of the "citizens'" movement to nominate a ticket and contest the seats of some delegates that took part in the preliminary meeting Saturday. If they fall to be seated, according to reports late Saturday to the central committee organized at the first meeting, they will bolt the convention and organize a rival convention to nominate the "citizens' " ticket. These reports were a matter of some concern Saturday to the central committee, but members of the committee this morning felt assured the original delegates would hold their seats. The new delegates, it is said, will be elected by citizens who did not take part in the election of the first delegates and the new claimants to seats will insist the original delegates were not chosen at meetings representative of their wards. Members of the central committee who are original delegates felt assured Saturday afternoon that they would not be unseated. "The ward meetings were regularly called," said Max Meyer of the Sixth ward. "Citizens from the various wards came out and elected their delegates. Plenty of notice was given everybody." It is part of the central committee's duty to see that meetings are held before next Saturday in wards where no meetings were held last week to elect delegates, but the committee was instructed that original delegates are to remain delegates to the permanent organization convention. All the members of the central committee of thirteen but two were at the meeting Saturday afternoon in the office of O. W. Gillespie, in the Dundee building. The absent members were Hub Diggs of the Twelfth ward. Plan of Nomination. The committee will recommend to the convention at the Chamber of Commerce building next Saturday morning at 10 o'clock that a nominating committee composed of one member from each ward and organization be formed. The organizations to be represented equally with the wards are the Trades Assembly, the Chamber of Commerce and the Young Men's Business League. The nominating committee will have sixteen members. This committee is to frame the list of candidates for mayor and other city offices and is to report the ticket back to the convention for ratification on the second Saturday from the day of the convention, which will make the day for ratification Aug. 29. Committee Recommendations. The recommendation of the central committee for the organization of the nominating committee will be presented by the resolutions committee of the central committee at the convention next Saturday. The central committee will recommend that the organization be made permanent and that V. S. Wardlaw be elected secretary. It will recommend that the temporary chairman appoint a committee of five on credentials. It is in the proceedings of this committee that the fight for the seating of rival delegations will come. The central committee will recommend further this order of business: Appointment of credentials committee. Vote on resolutions of central committee for permanent organization. Each ward and each of the three public bodies that have gone into the movement is entitled to four delegates to the convention next Saturday, making a total of sixty-four. It was the sense of the central committee Saturday afternoon that the "ticket" to be named by the movement will be merely for city offices. A meeting of the citizens of the Second ward has been called by W. F. Lowry, J. H. Waltam, Tom Colvin. The first four are original delegates from the Second ward and Colvin is the Second ward member. The meeting will be held in the west basement of the courthouse Monday night at 8 o'clock.

headlines.title: LANE SENDS OF CONTEST
headlines.fullText: Special to The Star-Telegram, AUSTIN, Aug. 15. Claiming that he was elected congressman-at-large over Jeff McLemore of Houston, who was awarded the nomination by the state convention at El Paso, W. P. Lane, through his attorneys, tonight sent to Fort Worth his contest petition to be filed with Cato Sells of that place, chairman of the state Democratic executive committee. The hearing of the petition is to be held here within twenty days as is provided by law. In his petition the contestant alleges that the votes of Saba, Oldham, Menard, Reagan and Dimmitt counties, if included in the official count, would have given him a majority of 655 votes over McLemore. It is further claimed that the state executive committee had knowledge of the votes of these counties, but refused to count them. The returns sent to the convention showed that McLemore received 98,959 and Lane 98,800. In the event the votes from these counties had been included in the official count, the vote would have been McLemore 99,414, Lane 100,069. It is further claimed that the vote of Zapata county, if ascertained, would still further increase his majority. The petitioner also claims that in Colorado and Starr counties the ballots cast were without the pledge required by statute. Of these alleged illegal votes in Colorado county, McLemore received 375 and Lane 236. The contestant prays that these illegal votes be thrown out, which would give a majority for Lane of 2,147 over McLemore. The petition is signed by Lane himself, Batts & Brooks, Judge T. C. Gaines of Austin, and Stanley Boykin of Fort Worth.

headlines.title: South Side Girl and G. G. Hayes to Wed September 2
headlines.fullText: The engagement of Miss Margaret Lois Lane, daughter of Hon. and Mrs. Hunter P. Lane of College avenue, to Mr. Gerald Grant Hayes was announced Monday at a party given by Miss Geneva Trammell of Lipscomb street. The wedding will be celebrated at the bride's home Wednesday, Sept. 2.

headlines.title: Heart Interest and Even Humor in Flood Scenes
headlines.fullText: Incidents full of heart interest and some of a humorous turn featured the day Saturday when the sudden rise in the Clear Fork threatened many. For Instance: Mrs. Swearington, living in one of the houses in the Valley View addition, attempted to use a feather bed as a life buoy for herself and three grown daughters, but the current was so swift that it was carried away. They had to swim to a wagon when it came later to take them away. Mrs. Will Mastus held a bundle of dry clothing above her head as she waded out of her home. The water was nearly to her neck. She went to an early neighbor's home and put on the dry clothes and then returned and watched the others as they were rescued from their homes. E. L. Stewart of Valley View had a card of meat which he had bought cheap and was saving for winter. It was washed away. Chickens Marooned. About forty chickens were marooned on top of the E. N. Payne grocery store, White Settlement road, all night long. Many pets perished. Dogs which were swimmers lost their lives in the battle against the swift current in efforts to reach high ground. A small boy went to the rescue of a dog which was battling against the current. The dog swam a distance of 300 feet before he got tangled up in a wire fence. R. F. Branch saw the dog and the small boy darted out from a camp of refugees in the street and waded water up to his waist to save the dog. A negro boy was seen riding on a log. The log was being carried down at a rapid rate and would swirl around and around occasionally when it struck a whirlpool, but the boy had his feet clutched around the timber and stuck there. He had not lost his balance up to the time he disappeared around the bend at the Seventh street bridge. Habe and Mother in Danger. Mrs. Vickers, lay in bed with her two days old baby without warning. Mrs. Vickers shouted for help. Her mother, Mrs. Janie Capps, 53, was in a state of near-prostration when men finally arrived in a wagon and carried them out to it. T. T. Trotter carried his crippled father, M. B. Trotter, out of his house and through two blocks of waist-deep water to the White settlement bridge. The elder Trotter could not walk. John Carmichael did rescue work with a horse and buggy. He found the children mounted on chairs and standing in water at that, in their home. He took them out through a window. Mrs. Anderson and her daughter with a baby were rescued by a man on a horse. Penny carried out several children. Women Forced to Wade. Several women in Valley View addition and Van Zandt addition women waded in water almost to their necks. Six women and eleven children made up a party of refugees from Van Zandt addition, who were grouped in the shelter of box cars on the Frisco dump near the Seventh street crossing late Saturday afternoon. They had to run for their lives and had not had anything to eat since morning. A peachy pearby grocer to give them food until Monday. The family of a man named Woods, a horse trader, living in a tent in the bottom near Van Zandt addition, would have been drowned but for the arrival of Arthur Hill and another man on a horse. Young Mrs. Woods was standing on a chair, with two tiny babies clasped to her breast, and screaming for help when Hill arrived. The water was around her waist, and rapidly rising. Hill took them from the home and took them to the Frisco track. The horse had to swim part of the way. Then he went back and rescued Woods, who was marooned on top of a wagon.

headlines.title: HUNDREDS HOMELESS AFTER FLOOD
headlines.fullText: (CONTINUED FROM PAGE ONE) o'clock Saturday morning to waken all the people in the flood district. "Every year I pay $5 taxes for this levee and now I get my home ruined," said George. "The city ought to give us back the money we have paid for levee taxes," complained E. L. Stewart. "The water would not have come up like this if the levee had not been cut. I was on the White Settlement road here when I looked up and saw a wall of water coming. That was the first warning we had."

headlines.title: LUMBERMEN PAID LITTLE IS REPORTED
headlines.subtitle: Investigation Shows Workers Forced to Live Under Bad Conditions.
headlines.fullText: Special to The Star-Telegram, SEATTLE, Aug. 15. - Stories of alleged exploitation of lumber employes were told today before the Federal industrial relations commission. At noon the hearing adjourned and the commission went to a nearby camp to make a personal inspection. Rev. Oscar H. McGill of Seattle, who visits camps frequently, declared that with some exceptions the men were housed under unsanitary conditions and every effort was made to pay them as little money as possible. "It has been testified here that the men sleep on mattresses without any change of windows" he said. "If you could see some of these mattresses you would not be surprised. I know of a camp where the men fight each night for a place on the table in preference to their bunks." Frequent changes in employes at the camps has led to reports of collusion between foremen and employers, and McGill said he believed the stories were well founded. Rudolph Diasler, a Cosmopolis, Wash. real estate man, gave similar testimony. He said the clothing of the men frequently was held for several weeks for debt, while they made futile attempts to obtain enough money to recover it.

headlines.title: Von Emmich Took Own Life Because of Liege Failure
headlines.fullText: LONDON, Aug. 15. - The Brussels correspondent of the London Telegraph says that he received positive confirmation that General Von Emmich, the German commander at Liege, shot and killed himself. The correspondent quotes a Belgian sergeant who was told of the suicide by a German officer whom he captured. "I know that Liege is to be my tomb, but I prefer to die by my own hand," is the substance of a message left by the German general to be transmitted to his wife. The German officer also said that eight German soldiers killed themselves on learning of the general's suicide.

headlines.title: FIGHT ON I.C.C RATE DECISION IS TO BE MADE
headlines.subtitle: Sam Cowan Goes to Conference With State Railroad Commissioners.
headlines.fullText: Special to The Star-Telegram, AUSTIN, Aug. 15. - Judge Sam H. Cowan, chairman of the traffic bureau of the Chamber of Commerce, will confer with Judge Allison Mayfield and Railroad Commissioner W. D. Williams left this afternoon for San Antonio. They will discuss the so-called Shreveport rate case with a view of framing a bill for the next legislature which will have the effect of giving some relief to the railroad commissions of the various states from the condition brought about by the recent decision of the United States Supreme court in this case. This conference with Judge Cowan will be held at the Gunter hotel. Judge Cowan left here Saturday to attend the meeting.

headlines.title: BAILEY MAY STATE HIS INTENTIONS
headlines.fullText: Former United States Senator Bailey is expected to state definitely in Fort Worth today whether he will be a candidate for the senate two years hence against Senator Culberson. He stated several days ago that he would make a definite announcement as to his candidacy either in Houston or in Fort Worth. He left Houston Saturday night at 11:45 o'clock without having made the statement and will arrive in Fort Worth this morning at 9:20 o'clock over the Houston & Texas Central. Senator Bailey denied in Houston Saturday that he had announced his candidacy, as had been reported. What he said was that he would be in the race for the United States senate if the resolutions in the El Paso convention condemning national prohibition and national woman suffrage were defeated. Again he sucked at the pipe. The crowd gave him an ovation while he spoke. In his speech he told the convention delegates he knew they would vote his resolutions down, not because they did not approve them, but because they were pledged to do so. Senator Bailey will be the guest today of Capt. S. B. Burnett. He will breakfast at Captain Burnett's home today at 1424 Summit avenue.

headlines.title: Claims Ft. Worth Worker It Means Rise of Slavs
headlines.subtitle: Employe at Packing House Puffs Old Cob Pipe and Dreams of Return to Power for His Race-Also, He Prepares Great Celebration in Home He Has Builded Here.
headlines.fullText: No matter what the outcome of the European war in its depths, the outcome will bring triumph to the firm Slav belief of Anton Koldin, 3101 Roosevelt avenue. "It means sunrise again for the Slavs," said Koldin, earnestly. "The fight is bringing brother against brother in battle array. The Germans are sending their men of Slavic breeding, led by Servians and Bohemians, against the Servians. Sixty per cent of the Hungarians are Slavonians. They detest the Austrian rule and expect, no matter what results, the Bohemians, Austrian Pollacks will be given home rule. It is a fight to get from under the 'under the hat rule?'" Koldin's light blue eyes gleamed. He sat in the plainest of clothes, among surroundings of which any hard working man might be proud. He Owns His Own Home. For Koldin has worked every year and almost every day since he came from Northern Bohemia to America. Most of his life has been spent among the cattle pens at the packing house of Armour & Co. He owns a well appointed brick home. Saturday night the dining room furniture had been cleared away. Koldin was preparing to entertain friends. It was to be a celebration in honor of the expected "sunrise for the Slavs." Squabs, chickens, bread and beer and other foodstuffs were sent to the home. More than fifty men, prominent in the big packing house departments were expected to attend. Men of all nationalities were to be there. "We can see nothing else," said Koldin. "The Slavs are to be powerful again. Austria will be a mere nothing. The Slavs of all the countries-of the dead nations of Bohemia, of Hungary, of independent Servia, Montenegro, Roumania and elsewhere will unite and become a power. Germany will fall and the Slav will once again take his place." And Koldin thoughtfully, but with no more vehemence, puffed at his pipe!

headlines.title: LAKE WORTH DAM STANDS HARD TEST
headlines.fullText: The dam at Lake Worth, on the West Fork, stood the test Saturday. It bore almost the heaviest pressure of water possible and not the least particle gave way. False reports were circulated on the streets that the dam had broken. It was the dam's first real test. The lake was only two-thirds full of water, but the dam held back the bulk of the West Fork's flood and kept the flood disaster in Fort Worth down to a minimum. With telephone connections to Lake Worth cut off, Commissioner Blanks became alarmed when he heard the rumor of a broken dam Saturday afternoon. The road was impassable to vehicles, so he hired a horse. He found everything safe at the dam.

headlines.title: Suffering Widespread in Wake of Trinity Flood
headlines.fullText: Widespread suffering exists in the Trinity river bottoms following the flood of Saturday. People driven from their homes are without places to stay and many today were starting over in life with their entire possessions ruined or washed away. Numerous cases of illness among those living in the flood-district adds another serious phase to the situation. Prompt relief measures are declared necessary. Mrs. William Whiteside of Van Zandt addition was swept off her feet twice as she waded water up to her breast in her flight from her home with her husband. "Everything we have is lost," she said. "I don't know where the next meal will come from. My husband is out of work. Her home is one she had worked for years to pay for it, and when I made the last payment I quit. Now I will have to work again. The water was up to my floor before I knew it. I put the trunks and things on chairs, and then run out to put the chickens on the roof. When I got back everything in the house was floating. We had to leave right away." Mrs. J. W. Scott got out of her house with her five children just barely in time. She had to leave her furniture. Her husband is out of work. "I haven't even got car fare," said Mrs. Scott. The water was waist deep when young Mrs. C. M. Hartman, with her sick baby in her arms, waded out of her house, making her way through a field to the Frisco track. She nearly fainted when she reached safety. "My husband has been out of work a month and everything we had is lost. The Lord only knows what we will do for a place to sleep. Lord have mercy on my sick baby." Mrs. D. H. Maben clasped her two-months-old baby to her breast, and was leading another child when she waded to safety. The water was coming up so rapidly she did not try to save anything but her children. The Maben family lost everything. Paralyzed Man Rescued. A paralyzed man had to be carried from his bed and was found in the nick of time. His family has suffered several recent misfortunes. Mrs. Willis draws a small salary at a laundry, and her two sons make $4 a week at the packing house. Gladys Jenkins, daughter of Dave Jenkins, lifted her mother off her sick bed and carried her out of the house. The water had risen to a point where the sick woman could dangle her hand off the bed and touch it. The work of years was undone when the water washed through the home of Sam Murray. Part of it was washed across the street. Children's tents and swings were washed away from almost all houses in the path of the flood. Five Children Hungry. Sam Murray's wife had a hard time to feed her five children through Sunday, but everything was lost when the water got into her home. The children went hungry Saturday. Mrs. L. H. Dowdy got her three children out of her home in plenty of time, but "I don't know what we will do for a place to sleep and something to eat," she said. Aged Invalid Suffers. After Mrs. F. A. Owens, 75 years of age, and almost an invalid, had been saved from her sick bed, it was hours before anybody could be found to take care of her. Her son, A. B. Owens, who lives at another home, spoke bitterly of her treatment. A man who had agreed to carry his mother to the White Settlement road, in her feebleness, Mrs. Owens may suffer fatal consequences from the exposure and suffering which she endured. The family is entirely out of money.

headlines.title: PURIFICATION OF WATER IS URGED
headlines.fullText: A warning to boil all city water-whether for bath or table use-in most of Fort Worth during the next few days was issued from the waterworks office Saturday. The office also urges everybody to boil it before drinking. Chlorine will be used in the purification process at the city filtration plant. This may give a peculiar odor which the water may have. While the waterworks office realizes that citizens will be complaining of river water, it is pointed out that if the water in the river had risen a few feet higher the Holly plant would have been put out of commission entirely and the city's supply of water would have been cut off.

headlines.title: Free Milk Fund Nurse Will Aid Relief Work
headlines.fullText: The Star-Telegram Free Milk and Ice Fund, insofar as its character will permit, will be used to relieve the suffering of the hundreds left homeless and destitute as a result of the Trinity river flood Saturday. The free nurse for the fund will devote her time today to studying conditions in the flood district, restoring order in place of the chaos caused by the waters, and furnishing relief where she can. The contributions to the fund can be used, of course, only to furnish milk and ice to the needy, and extra calls for those will be received by the nurse on the ground Sunday. Many of the sufferers didn't have much, but the little was their all, and that is sweetest. The report of the nurse, whose work here for two years has given her an intimate acquaintanceship with the poverty-stricken portion of the affected district, will be published in The Star-Telegram Monday afternoon.

events.eventName: Convention for permanent organization of the "citizens'" movement
Meeting of the citizens of the Second ward
